metadata.title: Connecting Action Semantics and Human Motion using Fuzzy Qualitative Kinematics
affiliations: School of Computer Science and Technology, Beijing Institute of Technology, China

abstract: Human motion understanding is fundamental to various applications in computer graphics, human-robot interactions, digital environments, and smart healthcare. Existing approaches predominantly rely on quantitative and qualitative kinematic facts for motion modeling. However, they often struggle to establish a robust connection between motion and corresponding action semantics. This is primarily attributed to the use of inadequate motion representations and presence of uncertainties in kinematic hierarchies. We propose a novel fuzzy qualitative kinematics (FQK) approach to effectively bridge action semantics and human motion. Our approach exploits the fusion of fuzzy inference and qualitative reasoning to model expressive, complex, and uncertain kinematic facts. First, we construct fuzzy linguistic vocabulary and membership functions for motion attributes. Second, we extract novel fuzzy qualitative tokens (FQTs) from motion sequences by employing FQK to represent various motion attributes across different motion components. Third, we propose a dual-branch adaptive neuro-fuzzy inference (D-ANFI) block. Finally, we evaluate a unified motion understanding system on interpolation, prediction, generation, and prompt-related tasks using HumanML3D and KIT-ML.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.summary: Frames the semantic gap between raw human motion and action semantics and motivates fuzzy qualitative tokens as an uncertainty-aware intermediate representation.
key_claims: Existing qualitative schemes rely on boolean or thresholded kinematic facts.
FQTs are proposed to better capture uncertainty, direction, amplitude, and velocity changes.
The approach targets interpolation, prediction, generation, and fine-grained prompt-based control.

sections.title: Related Work
sections.summary: Reviews human motion representation and motion generation literature, emphasizing intermediate representations and generative modeling families.

sections.title: Method
sections.summary: Defines fuzzy linguistic vocabularies, Gaussian membership functions, qualitative state functions, 11 fuzzy qualitative token families, a token extractor, a fuzzy-learning description, and a unified transformer-VAE-style motion understanding model.

sections.title: Implementation Details
sections.summary: Reports optimization settings, latent size, hardware, datasets, and task-specific inference descriptions.

sections.title: Kinematic Prompt Generation
sections.summary: Describes conversion of FQTs into text prompts and reports a prompt dataset of 1,548 items.

sections.title: Experiments
sections.summary: Evaluates the method on HumanML3D and KIT-ML with FID, Diversity, Multimodality, Accuracy, and R-Precision, plus qualitative examples, user study, and ablations.

sections.title: Conclusion
sections.summary: Reiterates the promise of FQTs for bridging motion and semantics and claims strong benchmark performance.

sections.title: Limitations and Future Work
sections.summary: Notes trade-offs in fuzzy vocabulary size, additional attributes, additional tokens, and translation ambiguity.

notes: PDF text was extractable, but the source paper contains internal inconsistencies that limit normalization fidelity.
The manuscript states 11 FQTs in the method but says the extractor uses ten types elsewhere.
Token names in Table I and the ablation section do not match the main method nomenclature.
An unresolved citation placeholder '[REF]' appears in the body text.
Several references appear duplicated or inconsistently normalized.
